longTailKeywords: B520 ICD-10 quartan malaria nephrotic syndrome
_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against WHO Guidelines for the Treatment of Malaria (3rd edition, 2024), published literature on P. malariae-associated nephropathy, and tropical medicine references. P. malariae causes a milder form of malaria than P. falciparum but is uniquely associated with chronic glomerulonephritis through immune complex deposition.

h1Title: Plasmodium malariae Malaria with Nephropathy: Symptoms, Treatment, and Prognosis
heroOverview: Plasmodium malariae malaria with nephropathy (ICD-10: B52.0) is a chronic form of malaria caused by Plasmodium malariae, the least virulent of the four human malaria parasites, which is uniquely associated with immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis leading to nephrotic syndrome. Unlike P. falciparum, which causes acute severe disease, P. malariae establishes a low-grade chronic infection characterized by quartan fevers (occurring every 72 hours) and gradual onset of nephropathy over years to decades of untreated infection. The nephropathy is mediated by antigen-antibody immune complexes that deposit in the glomerular basement membrane, triggering complement activation and progressive glomerulosclerosis. Without treatment, chronic P. malariae infection leads to the quartan nephrotic syndrome — a slowly progressive kidney disease characterized by heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, and eventual renal failure.

keyStats.prevalence: P. malariae accounts for approximately 1–3% of malaria cases globally, though prevalence is higher in some areas of West Africa and Oceania where asymptomatic low-grade infections persist; nephropathy develops in approximately 5–10% of untreated chronically infected individuals
keyStats.demographics: Occurs in all age groups but nephropathy typically manifests in adults 20–40 years after years of untreated infection; no significant gender predominance; endemic in tropical regions of sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Western Pacific
keyStats.avgAge: Nephropathy typically manifests 10–30 years after initial P. malariae infection; acute malaria symptoms (if any) may have occurred in childhood, with nephropathy appearing in adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 1–3 million P. malariae infections occur annually; the nephrotic syndrome variant (quartan nephrotic syndrome) is much rarer — estimated 50,000–100,000 cases globally, primarily in West Africa

definition: Plasmodium malariae infection with nephropathy (ICD-10: B52.0) is a chronic malaria presentation in which the P. malariae parasite has established a persistent low-grade infection that, over years, triggers an immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis. P. malariae is transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes and infects red blood cells with a 72-hour erythrocytic cycle — producing the characteristic quartan fever pattern (fever spikes every 3 days, as opposed to the tertian pattern of P. vivax and P. falciparum). The parasite can persist at subpatent levels (undetectable by standard microscopy) for decades, maintained by the immune system in a state of equilibrium. The nephropathy of P. malariae is a classic immune complex glomerulonephritis — parasite-derived antigens form circulating immune complexes with host antibodies, which deposit along the glomerular basement membrane, activate complement (via the alternative pathway), and cause a membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) with a characteristic 'tram-track' appearance on electron microscopy. The MPGN progresses to glomerulosclerosis and interstitial fibrosis over years, ultimately causing nephrotic syndrome and chronic kidney disease.

typesClassification.type: Subclinical chronic P. malariae infection with early nephropathy
typesClassification.description: Patients who acquired P. malariae infection (often in childhood) but remain asymptomatic or have only mild intermittent symptoms; over years, they develop evidence of renal involvement — microscopic hematuria, low-level proteinuria — detectable only on laboratory testing. This stage precedes overt nephrotic syndrome by months to years and may be the only stage amenable to curative treatment.

The fully developed nephropathy from chronic P. malariae infection — characterized by nephrotic-range proteinuria (above 3.5 g/day), hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia, and lipiduria. The MPGN lesion is well-established with characteristic electron microscopy findings (subendothelial deposits, tram-track GBM). This stage is often irreversible despite malaria treatment.

typesClassification.type: Chronic kidney disease secondary to P. malariae (CKD stage 3–5)
typesClassification.description: The end-stage manifestation of quartan nephrotic syndrome — progressive glomerulosclerosis and interstitial fibrosis have caused a significant reduction in GFR. Patients present with elevated creatinine, reduced estimated GFR, hypertension, and may be approaching or have reached end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation.

typesClassification.type: P. malariae and P. falciparum co-infection with nephropathy
typesClassification.description: Concurrent infection with P. malariae and P. falciparum — the more virulent P. falciparum causes acute malaria symptoms that bring the patient to medical attention, while the co-existing P. malariae (which may be subpatent) contributes to the development of nephropathy. Treatment of both species is required. Co-infection may accelerate nephropathy progression.

primarySymptoms: Quartan fever pattern — fever spikes occurring every 72 hours (every 3 days, hence 'quartan') rather than the more frequent pattern of P. falciparum (every 48 hours); the fever typically occurs in the afternoon or evening and lasts 8–12 hours, accompanied by chills, rigors, and profuse sweating.

Edema — periorbital edema in the morning and dependent edema (legs, ankles) by evening; reflects hypoalbuminemia from nephrotic-range proteinuria; may be subtle in early nephropathy but becomes prominent in quartan nephrotic syndrome.
Heavy proteinuria — urine that is frothy or foamy from high protein content; on urinalysis, proteinuria exceeds 3.5 g/day (nephrotic-range); patients may report noticing foam in urine.
Fatigue and malaise — from chronic low-grade infection combined with the effects of hypoalbuminemia; patients are oftenchronically fatigued but may attribute it to other causes.

Weight gain from fluid retention — patients with nephrotic syndrome gain weight from fluid accumulation rather than fat; this may be mistaken for simple weight gain without recognition of its renal significance.

Recurrent or persistent malaria symptoms — intermittent fever, chills, headache, and malaise occurring every few weeks or months; P. malariae can cause clinical relapses even after long periods of dormancy.
Hypertension — blood pressure elevation, often mild to moderate (140–160/90–100 mmHg), developing as nephropathy progresses; may be the presenting feature of chronic kidney disease in some patients.
Hematuria — tea-colored or cola-colored urine from microscopic or gross hematuria; the MPGN lesion causes red blood cells to leak into the urine, often with RBC casts indicating glomerular bleeding.

Decreased urine output in advanced disease — as GFR falls in advanced CKD, urine output may decrease; patients may notice reduced frequency of urination or nocturia from impaired concentrating ability.

Ascites and pleural effusions in severe nephrotic syndrome — massive proteinuria and hypoalbuminemia cause third-spacing of fluid into the peritoneal cavity (ascites) and pleural space (pleural effusions), particularly in the quartan nephrotic syndrome stage.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent or recurrent fevers every 72 hours in a patient from or with travel history to a malaria-endemic region
Foamy urine suggesting significant proteinuria, even in the absence of other symptoms
Edema (periorbital, peripheral) in a patient from a malaria-endemic region, particularly if accompanied by proteinuria
Unexplained microscopic hematuria and proteinuria in patients with a history of malaria or living in endemic areas
Elevated serum creatinine with proteinuria in patients with remote history of malaria
emergencySigns: Severe edema with respiratory distress suggesting massive fluid overload or pleural effusion — may require urgent nephrology evaluation and diuresis
Acute kidney injury superimposed on chronic P. malariae nephropathy — dehydration, nephrotoxic medications, or intercurrent illness can precipitate acute-on-chronic renal failure
Hyperkalemia with cardiac arrhythmias — advanced CKD causes potassium retention; severe hyperkalemia (K+ above 6.5 mEq/L) can cause life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias

Severe malaria parasitemia with renal failure — if P. malariae infection is accompanied by high parasitemia (rare but possible, especially with co-infection), severe malaria with renal involvement requires urgent treatment
Hypertensive emergency with renal involvement — severely elevated blood pressure in the context of P. malariae nephropathy requires urgent management

causes.description: P. malariae is transmitted by infected Anopheles mosquitoes. Unlike P. falciparum and P. vivax, P. malariae establishes a low-grade persistent infection in red blood cells — parasites can remain at subpatent levels for decades, maintained by a delicate balance between parasite replication and host immune control. Chronic P. malariae infection is the prerequisite for nephropathy development; untreated chronic infection over years to decades causes the immune complex nephritis.

causes.cause: Immune complex formation and glomerular deposition
causes.description: P. malariae-derived antigens form circulating immune complexes with host antibodies (primarily IgG). These immune complexes deposit along the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) and in the mesangium, triggering complement activation (primarily via the alternative pathway), recruitment of inflammatory cells, and mesangial proliferation. The characteristic electron microscopy finding is subendothelial deposits causing a 'tram-track' or double-contour appearance of the GBM.

The nephropathy requires ongoing antigen exposure to maintain immune complex formation. P. malariae's ability to persist at low levels for decades provides the chronic antigenic stimulus needed for progressive immune complex deposition. Effective antimalarial treatment can halt further antigen production, but existing immune complexes may continue to cause glomerular injury for some time.

Only a subset (5–10%) of patients with chronic P. malariae infection develop nephropathy, suggesting individual susceptibility factors. HLA associations (HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3 in some populations) have been described, similar to other immune complex glomerulonephritides. The role of other genetic polymorphisms (complement factors, Fc receptors) in determining who develops nephropathy is under investigation.

Concurrent infection with P. falciparum or P. vivax may accelerate the progression of P. malariae nephropathy — the more intense immune activation from P. falciparum infection increases overall immune complex burden and may increase deposition in the kidney. Co-infection also complicates diagnosis and treatment.

Patients in endemic areas who experience repeated P. malariae infections without adequate treatment are at highest risk for developing nephropathy. Each new infection and the resulting antigen-antibody complexes add to the cumulative glomerular immune complex burden. Access to prompt and effective treatment reduces the cumulative risk.

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged untreated or inadequately treated P. malariae infection
riskFactors.description: The risk of nephropathy increases with the duration of chronic P. malariae infection. Patients who acquired infection in childhood and received no or inadequate treatment are at the highest risk — nephropathy typically manifests 10–30 years after initial infection in these individuals.

P. malariae is endemic in West Africa (particularly Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon), Central and West Papua New Guinea, and parts of Southeast Asia. Individuals living in these areas have lifetime exposure to P. malariae and higher cumulative risk of chronic infection.

riskFactors.factor: Limited access to healthcare and antimalarial treatment
riskFactors.description: In areas with limited healthcare access, P. malariae infections often go untreated or are treated inadequately with substandard drugs, allowing the parasite to persist. Improving access to prompt diagnosis and effective treatment (ACT — artemisinin-based combination therapy) can prevent chronic infection and nephropathy.

Certain HLA haplotypes (HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3 in some studies) are associated with increased risk of developing nephropathy in the setting of chronic P. malariae infection. Family members of patients with quartan nephrotic syndrome may have elevated risk.
Co-infection with P. falciparum adds to the overall immune complex burden and may accelerate nephropathy progression. Adequate malaria prevention (ITNs, chemoprophylaxis) reduces co-infection risk.

Repeated P. malariae infections in individuals who have already established chronic infection add to the cumulative antigen load and immune complex deposition. Consistent use of ITNs and indoor residual spraying reduces re-infection rates.

affectedDemographics: P. malariae with nephropathy occurs primarily in children and young adults in endemic areas who acquired infection early in life and have had it for decades — the median age at nephrotic syndrome presentation is 20–40 years
No significant gender predominance; both males and females are equally affected
The highest prevalence of P. malariae and quartan nephrotic syndrome is in West Africa — particularly Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon, and surrounding countries
P. malariae is also endemic in Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and parts of Indonesia and Southeast Asia; these areas also have quartan nephrotic syndrome cases, though at lower rates
In non-endemic countries, P. malariae nephropathy occurs in migrants from endemic areas and in travelers returning from endemic regions with inadequately treated infection
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of P. malariae malaria with nephropathy requires confirmation of P. malariae infection and demonstration of renal involvement. Microscopy of Giemsa-stained thick and thin blood films identifies P. malariae by its characteristic 72-hour erythrocytic cycle and the mature schizonts containing 6–12 merozoites (vs. 12–24 in P. falciparum). PCR testing is more sensitive than microscopy for detecting low-level P. malariae parasitemia. Renal involvement is assessed by urinalysis (proteinuria, hematuria), 24-hour urine protein quantification, serum albumin, serum creatinine, and eGFR. Kidney biopsy is the definitive diagnostic test — it demonstrates the MPGN pattern with subendothelial deposits and the characteristic 'tram-track' appearance of the GBM on electron microscopy. Kidney biopsy is indicated when the diagnosis is uncertain or when the degree of proteinuria warrants immunosuppressive therapy. Clinical assessment by a specialist is recommended for confirmation and management. Early recognition and appropriate treatment improve outcomes and reduce complications. Early recognition and appropriate treatment improve outcomes and reduce complications.

diagnosticTests.test: Microscopy of Giemsa-stained blood films (thick and thin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies P. malariae parasites in peripheral blood. P. malariae is identified by mature schizonts containing 6–12 merozoites (vs. 12–24 in P. falciparum), absence of multiple infected RBCs (crescents), and the characteristic band-form (elongated) trophozoites. Sensitivity is limited for low-level parasitemia; multiple samples may be needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Finger-prick blood sample spread on a slide, stained with Giemsa, and examined under microscopy. Results within 2–4 hours. Parasite density is quantified if positive. May need repeat testing if initial negative but clinical suspicion is high.

diagnosticTests.test: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for malaria species identification
diagnosticTests.purpose: More sensitive than microscopy for detecting low-level P. malariae parasitemia — PCR can detect parasite DNA at densities below the threshold of microscopy (as low as 0.02 parasites/µL). Essential for confirming P. malariae infection in patients with negative microscopy but clinical suspicion of P. malariae nephropathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw (typically 5–10 mL in EDTA tube); sent to a reference laboratory or performed on-site if molecular diagnostics are available. Results in 1–3 days. Can distinguish all four human malaria species and detect mixed infections.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Point-of-care immunochromatographic test detecting malaria antigens (HRP2 for P. falciparum, pLDH for Pan-malaria and P. vivax/P. malariae). RDTs are less sensitive than PCR for low-level parasitemia and cannot distinguish P. malariae from P. vivax (they detect a common antigen). Useful for initial screening but PCR should be used to confirm P. malariae in suspected cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Finger-prick blood; results available in 15–30 minutes. A positive result indicates current malaria infection; species identification is limited. Negative RDT does not exclude low-level P. malariae infection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses renal involvement — proteinuria (detected by dipstick as 1+ to 4+), hematuria (RBCs on microscopy), and casts (RBC casts indicate glomerular bleeding). Quantified by 24-hour urine protein collection or spot protein/creatinine ratio. Nephrotic-range proteinuria exceeds 3.5 g/day.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Midstream clean-catch urine sample. Dipstick results within minutes; microscopic examination takes 30 minutes. 24-hour urine collection requires patient compliance over a full day.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum albumin, total protein, and lipid panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the nephrotic syndrome component — hypoalbuminemia (below 3.0 g/dL) from urinary protein loss; hyperlipidemia (elevated total cholesterol and triglycerides) from hepatic lipoprotein synthesis; these are the classic laboratory findings of nephrotic syndrome.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1–2 days. Low albumin and elevated lipids confirm the nephrotic component of the disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses kidney function — elevated creatinine and reduced eGFR indicate reduced glomerular filtration. P. malariae nephropathy typically causes progressive CKD over years; baseline eGFR and its trajectory over time guide prognosis and management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1–2 days. eGFR is calculated using the creatinine value, age, sex, and race (or using creatinine-based equations without race adjustment).

diagnosticTests.test: Kidney biopsy with light, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: The definitive diagnostic test for P. malariae nephropathy — demonstrates the MPGN pattern (mesangial hypercellularity, endocapillary proliferation, GBM thickening), immune complex deposits (IgG, C3), and the characteristic 'tram-track' appearance of the GBM on electron microscopy (due to subendothelial immune complex deposits). Also assesses the degree of glomerulosclerosis and interstitial fibrosis (irreversible damage).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound-guided percutaneous kidney biopsy (typically from the left lower pole) under local anesthetic. Takes 20–30 minutes. Risks include bleeding (hematuria, perinephric hematoma) and very rarely loss of renal function. Results from light microscopy, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy in 5–10 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Plasmodium malariae infection with nephropathy requires both antimalarial therapy and management of the renal complications. For uncomplicated P. malariae infection, chloroquine 10 mg per kg loading dose then 5 mg per kg at 24 and 48 hours is effective in most regions, though resistance patterns should guide choice. Alternatives include atovaquone-proguanil, mefloquine, or artemisinin-based combination therapies depending on regional resistance. Quinine plus doxycycline or tetracycline is used for severe cases or when oral therapy is not feasible. Management of nephropathy focuses on reducing proteinuria and slowing progression of kidney disease: angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers reduce proteinuria and provide renoprotection and are first-line when not contraindicated by hypotension or hyperkalemia. Dietary sodium and protein restriction is advised in advanced chronic kidney disease. Diuretics manage fluid overload. Erythropoietin-stimulating agents address anemia from chronic kidney disease when hemoglobin falls below 8 to 9 g per dL. Dialysis is required in end-stage renal disease. Hydroxychloroquine may reduce proteinuria in some patients with immune-complex glomerulonephritis associated with P. malariae infection. Monitoring of parasite clearance by blood smear and serial assessment of renal function is essential. Prevention with insecticide-treated bed nets and chemoprophylaxis in endemic regions reduces risk of infection and reinfection. Patient education about treatment adherence, monitoring for complications, and recognizing signs of disease progression is essential. Shared decision-making between the patient and treatment team improves outcomes and quality of life. Regular follow-up visits allow assessment of treatment response, management of adverse effects, and adjustment of therapy as needed. Multidisciplinary involvement including specialist physicians, nursing staff, social workers, and mental health professionals optimizes comprehensive care delivery.

medicalTreatments.name: Chloroquine phosphate (first-line for P. malariae)
medicalTreatments.description: The drug of choice for P. malariae malaria — chloroquine accumulates in parasitized red blood cells and is highly effective against P. malariae at all stages. Chloroquine is also anti-inflammatory, reducing some of the immunopathological components of the infection. Standard dosing: 10 mg base/kg initially, followed by 5 mg/kg at 24, 36, and 48 hours (total 25 mg base/kg over 3 days). Primaquine (0.25 mg base/kg) may be added to eliminate gametocytes and reduce transmission.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Highly effective for P. malariae — parasitological cure in over 95% of patients. Early treatment of P. malariae infection before it establishes chronic infection prevents nephropathy development. Treatment of established chronic infection halts further antigen production and immune complex deposition.

medicalTreatments.name: Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) for resistant P. malariae
medicalTreatments.description: ACT (artemether-lumefantrine, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine, artesunate-mefloquine) is recommended where chloroquine resistance is present or suspected. ACT rapidly clears parasites (artemisinin component) and provides longer-acting partner drug coverage (lumefantrine, piperaquine, mefloquine). Standard 3-day regimens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Highly effective against P. malariae — rapid clinical and parasitological response. Used when chloroquine resistance is suspected or confirmed. ACT is now first-line in many countries due to widespread chloroquine resistance even in P. malariae.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) or ARBs (losartan, valsartan) for proteinuria and CKD progression
medicalTreatments.description: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce proteinuria by decreasing intraglomerular pressure (via efferent arteriolar dilation) and reducing the permeability of the GBM to protein. They also slow the progression of CKD by reducing hyperfiltration and interstitial fibrosis. These are the cornerstone of nephropathy management in P. malariae-related CKD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce proteinuria by 30–50% and slow eGFR decline by approximately 30–50% in diabetic and non-diabetic CKD. First-line antihypertensive in patients with proteinuria. Should be titrated to maximal tolerated dose (e.g., lisinopril 20–40 mg daily or losartan 100 mg daily). Monitor potassium and creatinine after initiation.

medicalTreatments.name: Diuretics (furosemide, spironolactone) for edema management
medicalTreatments.description: Loop diuretics (furosemide) and potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone) manage the edema of nephrotic syndrome by promoting natriuresis. Spironolactone also has antiproteinuric effects and is used in combination with ACE inhibitors/ARBs for synergistic reduction of proteinuria.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for symptomatic edema — furosemide provides rapid diuresis, while spironolactone provides more modest but sustained effect. Combination therapy (furosemide + spironolactone) often works better than either alone for resistant edema.

medicalTreatments.name: Antihypertensive therapy (calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers) for hypertension control
medicalTreatments.description: Blood pressure control is critical in P. malariae nephropathy to slow CKD progression. ACE inhibitors/ARBs are preferred first-line agents, but calcium channel blockers (amlodipine) and beta-blockers (atenolol) are added when additional blood pressure control is needed. Target blood pressure is below 130/80 mmHg.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective blood pressure control reduces the rate of eGFR decline and reduces cardiovascular risk. Calcium channel blockers are particularly useful when ACE inhibitors/ARBs are contraindicated (e.g., bilateral renal artery stenosis).

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (prednisone) for refractory nephrotic syndrome
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose corticosteroids have been used in patients with severe, refractory nephrotic syndrome from P. malariae nephropathy, particularly when other causes of nephrotic syndrome have been excluded. The rationale is to suppress the inflammatory component of immune complex nephritis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence is limited — small case series suggest variable response. Corticosteroids may reduce proteinuria in some patients with MPGN pattern, but response is unpredictable. Consider a trial in refractory cases after ruling out other causes.

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney biopsy
surgicalOptions.description: While not a surgical treatment, kidney biopsy is a diagnostic procedure essential for confirming the diagnosis of P. malariae nephropathy, assessing the degree of irreversible damage (glomerulosclerosis, interstitial fibrosis), and guiding treatment decisions (e.g., eligibility for immunosuppressive therapy).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic yield of 95%+ when performed by experienced operators. Complications (bleeding) occur in approximately 1–3% of cases and are usually self-limited.

surgicalOptions.name: Renal replacement therapy (dialysis, kidney transplantation) for end-stage renal disease
surgicalOptions.description: When P. malariae nephropathy progresses to ESRD (eGFR below 10–15 mL/min/1.73m2), renal replacement therapy is required. Hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, or kidney transplantation are options. Kidney transplantation provides the best long-term outcomes but requires lifelong immunosuppression to prevent rejection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis effectively sustain life in ESRD. Kidney transplantation in patients with P. malariae-related ESRD has excellent outcomes — graft survival rates of 85–90% at 5 years. Recurrence of P. malariae nephropathy in the transplanted kidney is rare with appropriate antimalarial prophylaxis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Immunosuppressive therapy (cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate mofetil)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in severe P. malariae nephropathy with nephrotic syndrome unresponsive to antimalarial treatment and ACE inhibitors/ARBs. Evidence is limited to case reports and small series. Cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate may be considered as second-line agents when corticosteroids fail.

alternativeTreatments.name: Plasmapheresis
alternativeTreatments.description: Removal of circulating immune complexes by plasmapheresis has been attempted in severe, rapidly progressive P. malariae nephropathy with high levels of circulating immune complexes. Limited evidence for efficacy; considered experimental.

alternativeTreatments.name: Traditional herbal treatments
alternativeTreatments.description: Various traditional remedies are used in endemic areas for malaria and kidney symptoms. None have evidence of efficacy against P. malariae or its nephropathy and may cause harm. Patients should be counseled to use evidence-based treatments (antimalarials, ACE inhibitors) rather than traditional remedies.

whySeeSpecialist: Patients with P. malariae malaria with nephropathy require care from an infectious disease specialist for malaria treatment and a nephrologist for management of the renal involvement. In endemic areas, these specialists may work together with general physicians and community health workers. Patients with advanced CKD require a nephrologist for renal replacement therapy planning.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an infectious disease physician or tropical medicine specialist with experience in malaria and its complications. Ask whether they have managed P. malariae nephropathy specifically and whether they work with a nephrologist for renal management. In endemic countries, regional hospitals with tropical disease expertise often have the most experience with quartan nephrotic syndrome.
preventionStrategies: Prompt and effective treatment of P. malariae infections — preventing chronic infection is the most effective way to prevent nephropathy; ACT or chloroquine treatment at first infection stops the parasite from establishing a persistent infection
Insecticide-treated net (ITN) use — sleeping under ITNs prevents mosquito bites and malaria infection, reducing the risk of chronic P. malariae infection in endemic areas
Indoor residual spraying (IRS) — spraying the inside walls of houses with insecticide reduces mosquito populations and malaria transmission in endemic areas
Chemoprophylaxis for high-risk individuals — travelers to P. malariae-endemic areas and individuals with recurrent infections may benefit from prophylaxis with chloroquine or atovaquone-proguanil
Screening and treatment of asymptomatic carriers — in endemic areas, identifying and treating individuals with subpatent P. malariae infection may prevent future nephropathy development
lifestyleModifications: Low-sodium diet — sodium restriction reduces fluid retention and edema; recommended intake of less than 2 grams of sodium daily for patients with nephrotic syndrome and hypertension

Adequate protein intake with protein restriction in advanced CKD — adequate protein supports albumin synthesis in nephrotic patients but protein restriction may be needed in advanced CKD (eGFR below 30) to reduce nitrogenous waste accumulation

Avoidance of nephrotoxic medications — NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen), aminoglycosides, and high-dose aspirin should be avoided as they can accelerate CKD progression; all medications should be reviewed with a physician
Regular monitoring of kidney function — self-monitoring of blood pressure, weight, and urine output between clinical visits helps detect deterioration early
Preventive malaria measures — consistent use of ITNs and indoor residual spraying prevents re-infection, which would add to cumulative antigen burden

recommended: Low-sodium diet — sodium restriction (less than 2 g/day) reduces fluid retention and hypertension; avoid processed foods, canned soups, fast food; use herbs and spices instead of salt for flavoring

High-quality protein — adequate protein (0.8–1.0 g/kg/day) prevents muscle wasting and supports albumin synthesis; lean meats, fish, eggs, dairy, legumes; protein restriction to 0.6–0.8 g/kg/day may be needed in advanced CKD
Fresh fruits and vegetables — provides vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants that support overall health; particularly important in endemic areas where malnutrition is common
Adequate hydration — drinking sufficient water maintains renal perfusion (unless fluid restriction is needed in advanced CKD with oliguria)
avoid: High-sodium foods — processed meats, canned foods, salty snacks, soy sauce; excess sodium worsens hypertension and edema in nephrotic syndrome
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin above 81 mg) — NSAIDs reduce renal perfusion and can precipitate acute kidney injury in patients with CKD; use acetaminophen for pain instead
Excessive protein in advanced CKD — very high protein intake (>1.2 g/kg/day) accelerates CKD progression; protein intake should be moderate in stage 4–5 CKD

Potassium-rich foods in advanced CKD — when eGFR is below 30, potassium accumulation becomes a risk; avoid high-potassium foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, tomatoes) unless potassium-wasting diuretics are being used

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise (walking, gentle stretching) is beneficial for overall health and cardiovascular fitness in patients with stable P. malariae nephropathy and CKD. Strenuous exercise should be avoided if the patient is symptomatic (severe anemia, advanced CKD with fatigue) or has active malaria symptoms. Rest is appropriate during acute malaria exacerbations.
dailyManagement: Antimalarial medication adherence — complete the full course of antimalarial treatment as prescribed; incomplete treatment risks treatment failure and persistent infection
ACE inhibitor/ARB adherence — these medications are essential for reducing proteinuria and slowing CKD progression; do not stop without consulting a physician
Blood pressure monitoring — measure blood pressure regularly (at home or at a clinic); target below 130/80 mmHg; report any significant changes to the physician
Weight monitoring — weigh yourself daily or every other day; rapid weight gain suggests fluid retention and worsening nephrotic syndrome
Urine dipstick monitoring — if urine dipsticks are available, check for protein and blood regularly; significant changes warrant medical evaluation
Preventive malaria measures — use ITNs every night, re-treat nets as directed, and seek prompt treatment for any fever in an endemic area
prognosis: The prognosis of P. malariae malaria with nephropathy depends on the stage of the disease at diagnosis and the adequacy of treatment. Early-stage nephropathy (subclinical infection with mild proteinuria) has a good prognosis with antimalarial treatment and ACE inhibitors — progression to ESRD can be slowed or halted in many patients. Quartan nephrotic syndrome (established nephrotic-range proteinuria) has a worse prognosis — the glomerular damage is often advanced and progressive, and many patients develop ESRD within 5–10 years despite treatment. Early antimalarial treatment of P. malariae infection before chronic infection is established prevents nephropathy development and provides the best outcomes. The prognosis for P. malariae nephropathy is substantially worse than for other treatable causes of nephrotic syndrome (e.g., membranous nephropathy secondary to treatable causes) due to the chronic nature of the immune complex injury.
recoveryTimeline: Antimalarial treatment (chloroquine or ACT) clears P. malariae parasites from the blood — parasitological cure is achieved within days. However, the immune complex nephritis may continue to cause kidney damage for weeks to months after parasite clearance, as existing immune complexes slowly clear from the glomeruli. Proteinuria may improve over 3–6 months after effective antimalarial treatment in early-stage disease, but established nephrotic syndrome may not improve significantly. CKD progression, once established, is measured over years — eGFR declines at approximately 3–5 mL/min/1.73m2 per year without intervention, and 1–3 mL/min/1.73m2 per year with optimal treatment (ACE inhibitors, BP control). ESRD (requiring dialysis) typically develops 5–15 years after nephrotic syndrome onset in the absence of transplant.
complications: End-stage renal disease (ESRD) — the most serious complication of P. malariae nephropathy; progressive glomerulosclerosis and interstitial fibrosis reduce eGFR to below 10–15 mL/min/1.73m2, requiring dialysis or transplantation for survival; ESRD develops in approximately 30–50% of patients with quartan nephrotic syndrome over 10–15 years

Cardiovascular complications of nephrotic syndrome — severe hypoalbuminemia causes edema, increases thrombotic risk (renal vein thrombosis, DVT, PE), and contributes to hyperlipidemia and accelerated atherosclerosis; cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death in CKD patients

Infection complications — nephrotic syndrome impairs immune function (low IgG from urinary losses), increasing infection risk, particularly for encapsulated bacteria (Streptococcus pneumoniae); edema and skin breakdown increase skin infection risk
Malnutrition from protein loss — heavy proteinuria leads to hypoalbuminemia and muscle wasting; patients may become cachectic and more susceptible to intercurrent infections

Pulmonary edema from fluid overload — severe nephrotic syndrome causes massive fluid retention; pulmonary edema (fluid in the lungs) causes dyspnea and is a medical emergency; treated with oxygen, diuretics, and in severe cases, dialysis for ultrafiltration

Recurrent malaria exacerbations — chronic P. malariae infection can flare with decreasing immunity, causing recurrent quartan fevers; each flare adds to the cumulative antigen burden and may worsen nephropathy

supportResources.name: WHO — Guidelines for the Treatment of Malaria, 3rd edition, 2024
supportResources.description: Comprehensive evidence-based guidelines for malaria diagnosis and treatment, including P. malariae.

supportResources.name: Malaria Atlas Project — P. malariae prevalence data
supportResources.description: Global mapping of P. malariae prevalence and endemicity, useful for understanding the geographic distribution of quartan malaria.

supportResources.name: International Society of Nephrology — Resources for Kidney Disease in Low-Resource Settings
supportResources.description: Resources for managing kidney disease in endemic areas, including guidance on dialysis and transplantation in resource-limited settings.

confusedWithConditions.name: Severe Falciparum Malaria with Acute Kidney Injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Falciparum malaria can cause acute kidney injury through AKI (acute tubular necrosis from hypoperfusion, pigment nephropathy from hemolysis) — this is an acute, potentially reversible condition occurring in the context of severe P. falciparum infection. P. malariae nephropathy is a chronic, slowly progressive immune complex MPGN that develops over years of untreated infection and is not accompanied by the severe acute symptoms of P. falciparum malaria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary Membranous Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary (idiopathic) membranous nephropathy is an immune complex glomerulonephritis with unknown antigen (now known to be PLA2R in most cases) that produces a similar nephrotic syndrome to P. malariae nephropathy. The distinction is made by identifying P. malariae infection (PCR, microscopy), the characteristic 'tram-track' GBM appearance (more typical of MPGN than membranous), and the clinical context of endemic malaria exposure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease (unspecified cause)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CKD from P. malariae nephropathy is a specific cause of CKD — distinguished from other causes by the history of chronic P. malariae infection, the MPGN pattern on biopsy, and the geographical/clinical context. The management of P. malariae-related CKD has unique considerations (antimalarial treatment, immune complex management) compared to other CKD etiologies.

relatedConditions.name: Severe Falciparum Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: While P. malariae causes a milder acute malaria than P. falciparum, co-infection with P. falciparum adds to the immune complex burden and may accelerate P. malariae nephropathy. Management of co-infection requires treatment of both species — ACT is preferred for P. falciparum, and chloroquine or ACT for P. malariae.

relatedConditions.name: Membranoproliferative Glomerulonephritis (MPGN)
relatedConditions.relevance: MPGN is the histologic pattern of P. malariae nephropathy — the immune complexes deposit in the subendothelial space, causing GBM thickening and the characteristic 'tram-track' double contour. Other causes of MPGN (hepatitis C, cryoglobulinemia, lupus) should be excluded when diagnosing P. malariae nephropathy.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
relatedConditions.relevance: P. malariae nephropathy is a cause of CKD — it progresses through stages from early proteinuria to nephrotic syndrome to ESRD. The management principles (BP control, ACE inhibitors, diet, avoiding nephrotoxins) are similar to other causes of CKD, but the antimalarial treatment is a unique component of P. malariae-related CKD.

faqs.question: What is Plasmodium malariae malaria with nephropathy?
faqs.answer: P. malariae malaria with nephropathy is a chronic form of malaria caused by the P. malariae parasite that, over years of untreated infection, triggers an immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis leading to nephrotic syndrome and chronic kidney disease. P. malariae is the least virulent human malaria parasite — it causes milder symptoms (quartan fevers every 72 hours) but uniquely causes a progressive kidney disease through immune complex deposition in the glomeruli.

faqs.question: How is P. malariae different from other malaria parasites?
faqs.answer: P. malariae differs from P. falciparum, P. vivax, and P. ovale in several key ways: it causes milder symptoms (quartan fevers every 72 hours rather than the 48-hour cycle of other species); it can persist at very low levels in the blood for decades; and it is uniquely associated with chronic immune complex glomerulonephritis (quartan nephrotic syndrome). P. falciparum causes severe acute disease with high parasitemia and organ failure — very different from P. malariae's indolent chronic course.

faqs.question: How does P. malariae cause kidney disease?
faqs.answer: P. malariae-derived antigens form circulating immune complexes with host antibodies. These immune complexes deposit along the glomerular basement membrane, activate complement, and cause inflammation (mesangial proliferation, endocapillary proliferation). Over years, this membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) leads to glomerulosclerosis and progressive loss of kidney function. Only a subset (5–10%) of chronically infected patients develop this complication.

faqs.question: What is quartan nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Quartan nephrotic syndrome (QNS) is the fully developed nephropathy from chronic P. malariae infection — characterized by nephrotic-range proteinuria (more than 3.5 g/day), low albumin in the blood, edema, high cholesterol, and progressive loss of kidney function. The name comes from the quartan fever pattern of P. malariae and the nephrotic syndrome caused by the infection. It is distinct from other causes of nephrotic syndrome and is a complication specific to chronic P. malariae infection.

faqs.question: How is P. malariae with nephropathy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires: (1) confirming P. malariae infection via microscopy (identifying the characteristic schizont with 6–12 merozoites) or PCR; (2) demonstrating renal involvement via urinalysis (proteinuria, hematuria), blood tests (low albumin, elevated creatinine), and eGFR; and (3) optionally, kidney biopsy showing MPGN with 'tram-track' GBM appearance on electron microscopy.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for P. malariae nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Treatment has two components: antimalarial drugs (chloroquine or ACT) to clear the P. malariae infection and stop ongoing antigen production, and management of the kidney disease (ACE inhibitors or ARBs to reduce proteinuria and slow CKD progression, blood pressure control, diuretics for edema). In advanced cases, dialysis or kidney transplantation may be needed for end-stage renal disease.

faqs.question: Can P. malariae nephropathy be cured?
faqs.answer: The malaria infection can be cured with effective antimalarial treatment — this halts further antigen production and immune complex deposition. However, existing kidney damage may not be reversible. Early-stage nephropathy (with mild proteinuria) can be stabilized and may even improve with treatment. Established nephrotic syndrome is often progressive despite antimalarial treatment. Early detection and treatment of P. malariae infection before it causes nephropathy provides the best outcomes.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis of P. malariae nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Prognosis depends on the stage at diagnosis. Early-stage disease (mild proteinuria, normal eGFR) treated with antimalarial drugs and ACE inhibitors has a good prognosis — CKD progression can be slowed or halted. Quartan nephrotic syndrome (established nephrotic-range proteinuria) has a worse prognosis — approximately 30–50% of patients develop ESRD within 10–15 years. Early treatment of P. malariae infection before chronic infection is established prevents nephropathy entirely.

faqs.question: Why is P. malariae called 'quartan malaria'?
faqs.answer: P. malariae is called quartan malaria because its erythrocytic cycle is 72 hours (every 3 days), producing fever spikes that occur on the fourth day (hence 'quartan' — from the Latin 'quattuor' meaning four). This is in contrast to tertian malaria (P. vivax, P. falciparum) with a 48-hour cycle producing fevers every other day. The quartan fever pattern is characteristic of P. malariae and helps distinguish it clinically from other malaria species.

faqs.question: How common is P. malariae nephropathy?
faqs.answer: P. malariae causes approximately 1–3% of malaria cases globally, but only a small fraction of these develop nephropathy — estimated at 5–10% of patients with chronic untreated P. malariae infection. Quartan nephrotic syndrome is rare in global terms (approximately 50,000–100,000 cases) but represents a significant burden in endemic areas of West Africa and Oceania where P. malariae is common.

faqs.question: Can P. malariae be detected with standard rapid diagnostic tests?
faqs.answer: Standard RDTs detect a common antigen (pLDH) present in P. vivax, P. malariae, and P. ovale, but they cannot distinguish between these species. P. falciparum RDTs detect HRP2, which is specific to P. falciparum. Species identification requires microscopy (looking for the characteristic P. malariae morphology) or PCR (more sensitive for low-level parasitemia).

faqs.question: What does the kidney biopsy show in P. malariae nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Kidney biopsy in P. malariae nephropathy shows a membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) pattern — increased mesangial cells, endocapillary proliferation, and GBM thickening. Immunofluorescence shows deposits of IgG, IgM, and C3 along the capillary walls. On electron microscopy, the characteristic finding is subendothelial deposits causing a 'tram-track' or double-contour appearance of the GBM.

faqs.question: Is P. malariae infection common in travelers?
faqs.answer: P. malariae is relatively uncommon in travelers to non-endemic areas — most malaria cases in travelers are P. falciparum (approximately 75%) or P. vivax (approximately 15%). P. malariae accounts for less than 5% of imported malaria cases in non-endemic countries. Travelers to West Africa and Papua New Guinea are at highest risk for P. malariae infection.

faqs.question: What causes the quartan fever pattern?
faqs.answer: The quartan (every-3-day) fever pattern of P. malariae reflects the 72-hour erythrocytic cycle of the parasite — merozoites are released from infected red blood cells every 72 hours, triggering the fever response each time. This is in contrast to P. falciparum and P. vivax, which have 48-hour cycles and produce fever every other day (tertian pattern). The predictable timing of fever episodes is a clinical clue to P. malariae.

faqs.question: What is the 'tram-track' sign on kidney biopsy?
faqs.answer: The 'tram-track' sign is a double-contour of the glomerular basement membrane seen on electron microscopy in membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN). It results from subendothelial immune complex deposits that separate the basement membrane from the endothelial cell, creating the appearance of parallel tracks. This finding is characteristic of immune complex-mediated MPGN, including P. malariae nephropathy.

faqs.question: Does treating P. malariae reverse the kidney damage?
faqs.answer: Treating P. malariae with effective antimalarial drugs halts further immune complex deposition but does not reverse existing glomerular damage. In early-stage disease (mild proteinuria, early MPGN changes), some improvement in proteinuria and stabilization of eGFR is possible with antimalarial treatment plus ACE inhibitors. In established nephrotic syndrome with advanced glomerulosclerosis, reversal is not possible — treatment aims to slow further progression.

faqs.question: Is P. malariae resistant to chloroquine?
faqs.answer: Chloroquine resistance in P. malariae has been reported in some areas, particularly in Southeast Asia and Oceania, but remains less widespread than P. falciparum chloroquine resistance. In areas with known P. malariae chloroquine resistance, ACT (artemisinin-based combination therapy) is recommended. In most of sub-Saharan Africa, chloroquine remains effective against P. malariae.

faqs.question: Can P. malariae cause chronic kidney disease without nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes — P. malariae can cause progressive CKD without the full nephrotic syndrome picture. Some patients develop CKD with proteinuria below the nephrotic range (less than 3.5 g/day), hypertension, and declining eGFR. This may represent an earlier or more slowly progressive form of the same immune complex nephritis. ACE inhibitors and antimalarial treatment are still beneficial in these patients.

faqs.question: What is the role of kidney transplantation in P. malariae nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Kidney transplantation is the definitive treatment for ESRD from P. malariae nephropathy — it provides better long-term outcomes and quality of life than dialysis. The transplanted kidney can develop recurrent disease if P. malariae is not fully eradicated, but this is rare with appropriate antimalarial prophylaxis. Living donor transplantation is preferred where available.

faqs.question: How can P. malariae nephropathy be prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention of P. malariae nephropathy involves: preventing chronic P. malariae infection through consistent use of insecticide-treated nets and indoor residual spraying; prompt and effective treatment of P. malariae infections with chloroquine or ACT before they establish chronic infection; and in areas of known exposure, screening for and treating subpatent P. malariae infection before it causes renal damage. Early treatment is the most effective prevention strategy.
